story:
Harriet brandishes a cream-colored invitation that arrived in a company-logo envelope, scoffing at the paper waste and Philip Elton’s private-jet footprint. I’m more focused on coffee than carbon math and tease that Philip is rich, handsome, and single—until Harriet reminds me he was in love with me. He did propose; I turned him down. I’d tried to nudge him toward Harriet anyway, but he dismissed her with a cool line about “class,” which made me bristle. It made me see how Philip and I occupy the same glossy world—and how smug that world can be.

Our offices mirror us: Harriet’s tidy room with the coveted coffee machine and her gleaming stilettos; mine equally sized but at the far end of an oak-paneled corridor that always makes us feel like we ought to don wigs and gowns. Today we’re in regular clothes, me slightly prim next to her sparkle.

The invitation is for a lavish welcome-back party for Philip, now newly returned from New York to head Elton Corporation. Harriet wants reassurance. I give it, though I already know that when we reach the bank Philip will introdUce his wife. i conveNiently Skip menTioning how flat philip’s face went when i suggested he date her. Meanwhile, Harriet has a letter from Robert Martin, the hunky farmer she once liked. I tell her to bin it—then hear myself and realize I’m being unfair. Harriet isn’t a farm girl, true, but that doesn’t make anyone inferior. It just means her life fits somewhere else. She says she won’t write back. I say out of sight, out of mind, and unpin my chignon so my hair feels more “party” and less “courtroom.”

Elton Corp doesn’t do anything halfway. The venue is a restored Art Deco bank: marble, bronze, a staircase that demands a silk dress and old-Hollywood waves. When we arrive, everyone is dressed to the nines; it’s definitively black tie. On the steps, Philip looks like the poster boy for sexy billionaire CEOs. I keep my chin high, telling myself the “single” part won’t last—though not in any way Harriet would want.

We go to greet the host. Beside Philip stands a woman whose raven curls and scarlet silk dress could start a riot. She’s tall, poised, and unmistakably in charge. “Here come the smartest legal brains in London,” Philip says affably. “I’m glad you could make it, girls. Please meet my wife, Augusta Elton.”

Wife. The diamond on her hand grazes mine—hard—and Harriet wobbles. I steer her toward a passing tray and down two flutes of Bollinger like water. Harriet’s lips tremble, forming a stunned “Did he say—” Yes. He did.

The music swells. The gilt mirrors gleam. Augusta wears her triumph like couture; Philip looks coolly satisfied. Around us, London’s moneyed crowd buzzes, as if this reveal is just another item on the agenda. I’ve spent months assuming I could direct hearts like court filings—Philip toward Harriet, Harriet away from Robert—while pretending I’m immune. Now the game rearranges itself in one sentence and a glittering ring.

I tell Harriet to breathe, to drink, to wait before deciding anything. As Augusta’s smug smile flashes in the mirror, I mouth silently, We’ll see each other again, and slip with Harriet into the crowd, where everything suddenly looks sharper and far less simple.
